whatThisIs: Score the five-component Bishop score for cervical readiness before labor induction: dilation (0-3 points), effacement (0-3), station (0-3), cervical consistency (0-2), and cervical position (0-2), for a total of 0-13. Sophie Well returns the total with the standard interpretation: a score of 8 or higher predicts a vaginal delivery probability comparable to spontaneous labor; a score of 6 or lower (often described as an 'unfavorable cervix') is associated with longer induction times and higher cesarean rates and typically prompts cervical ripening before oxytocin.
whenToUse: Use this when planning a labor induction or when counseling a patient about expected induction success. The Bishop score is most useful for nulliparous patients - in multiparous patients with a history of vaginal delivery, even lower scores are commonly successful. Modified scoring schemas exist (modified Bishop, simplified Bishop) but the components are the same; use the version your hospital protocol references for documentation. The score guides ripening agent selection (mechanical vs. prostaglandin) and counseling but does not change the indication for induction.
